title: The Matter of Wonder: Abhinavagupta's Panentheism and the New Materialism
subtitle: Loriliai Biernacki's OUP monograph on Abhinavagupta's camatkāra (wonder), panentheism, and the liveliness of matter

summary.one_line: A sustained argument that Abhinavagupta's panentheism — centred on camatkāra (wonder), the subjectivity of matter, and the non-duality of consciousness and materiality — offers a rigorous philosoph...
summary.scope: Full monograph (OUP 2023). Chapter 1 outlines Abhinavagupta's panentheism and its relevance to New Materialism. Chapter 2 develops camatkāra (wonder) as the affective tone of reality. Chapter 3 examines the subjectivity of matter through Abhinavagupta's theory of reflection (pratibimbavāda). Chapter 4 compares panentheism, panpsychism, and theism in Abhinavagupta's framework. Chapter 5 applies the model to contemporary issues of ecology, animal consciousness, and the value of the material world.

sources.label: Biernacki, Loriliai. The Matter of Wonder: Abhinavagupta's Panentheism and the New Materialism. New York: Oxford University Press, 2023.
Abhinavagupta, Tantrāloka — cited throughout for panentheist ontology
Abhinavagupta's aesthetic theory (Abhinavabhāratī) — camatkāra as the foundation of aesthetic and spiritual experience

body.text: Abhinavagupta (950–1016) articulated a panentheism — both seeing the divine as immanent in the world and at the same time as transcendent — as a way to account for the wonder of material existence. For a New Materialism, Abhinavagupta's articulation of panentheism, centered around a foundational subjectivity, gives us a way to rethink matter as alive and valuable, extending our ethical consideration to animals, insects, worms — even down to a worm, as Abhinavagupta tells us. His panentheism postulates a single reality, Śiva, which unfolds out of itself the wonderful diversity of our world. Key elements: his use of wonder (camatkāra) serves to alter our vision of matter, pointing to its essential liveliness; his theory of reflection (pratibimbavāda) provides a model for how consciousness relates to materiality without reducing either; his panentheism reformulates the relationship between matter and consciousness by drawing on psychological, linguistic, and aesthetic categories.

Camatkāra — usually translated as 'wonder' or 'astonishment' — is the central aesthetic category in Abhinavagupta's theory of rasa. But Biernacki argues it is much more: camatkāra is the very texture of reality when experienced as the self-disclosure of consciousness. In Abhinavagupta's panentheism, the world is not dead matter but a wondrous expression of the divine. Camatkāra is not merely a human response to beauty — it is the ontological signature of reality itself. When we experience wonder, we are participating in the self-awareness of Śiva. This reframes aesthetic experience as a mode of theological and metaphysical cognition.

Abhinavagupta's doctrine of reflection (pratibimbavāda) holds that the world is a reflection in the mirror of Śiva's consciousness. Biernacki argues this is not idealism — matter is not illusory but is a real manifestation of consciousness, sharing in its reality. The reflection model preserves both the unity of the source (Śiva) and the reality of the reflected world. Unlike the Advaita Vedānta model where the world is a false appearance (vivartavāda), Abhinavagupta's reflection doctrine grants the world ontological weight while maintaining its dependence on consciousness. This makes it a promising resource for contemporary philosophy of mind and ecology.

Biernacki distinguishes Abhinavagupta's panentheism from both panpsychism and classical theism. Unlike panpsychism (which attributes mind to all matter but struggles to account for unity), Abhinavagupta's model posits a single consciousness that manifests as all subjects. Unlike classical theism (which separates God from world), panentheism holds that the world is in God and God is in the world without being exhausted by it. Abhinavagupta's formulation is distinctive: Śiva is both the material and efficient cause of the universe, and the universe is Śiva's body — but Śiva transcends the universe as its subjective ground.

Abhinavagupta's panentheism extends ethical consideration to all beings because all beings are manifestations of Śiva. As Biernacki notes, Abhinavagupta states that this applies 'even down to a worm.' The wonder (camatkāra) that characterises reality is not a human privilege but the intrinsic quality of all existence. This provides a metaphysical foundation for ecological ethics: if matter is inherently valuable as a manifestation of the divine, then the exploitation of nature is not merely imprudent but metaphysically mistaken. The New Materialist project of revaluing matter finds a powerful ally in Abhinavagupta's panentheism.

gaps: Full book is 10,677 lines — only key theses extracted. Chapters on comparative philosophy and ecology partially covered.

versions.changes: Initial extraction of key arguments from Biernacki's OUP monograph. Full text at /tmp/tantraloka3.txt
